name: CA
title: Canada Political Quiz 2026 | CoreOpinion
desc: Take the free Canadian political compass quiz. 47 policy questions and statements to find your position and check alignment with Liberal, Conservative, NDP, and more.
keywords: Canadian political quiz, Canada political compass test, Liberal Conservative NDP quiz, where do I stand in Canadian politics, political alignment test Canada, which party should I vote for Canada, Carney Poilievre quiz, Canadian election 2025 quiz

lib.name: Liberal
lib.desc: Your answers most closely match the Liberal Party. You tend to support managed trade, social programs, climate action, and a strong federal role in the economy.

con.name: Conservative
con.desc: Your answers most closely match the Conservative Party. You favour lower taxes, smaller government, axing the carbon tax, and reducing federal spending.

ndp.name: NDP
ndp.desc: Your answers most closely match the NDP. You support pharmacare, dental care, workers rights, wealth taxes, and a more activist government on inequality and climate.

grn.name: Green
grn.desc: Your answers most closely match the Green Party. You prioritise strong climate action, proportional representation, and a systemic challenge to corporate power.

blq.name: Bloc Québécois
blq.desc: Your answers most closely match the Bloc. You support Quebec autonomy, French language protection, and a centre-left social model on federal matters.

ppc.name: PPC
ppc.desc: Your answers most closely match the PPC. You want radical cuts to immigration, elimination of climate policy, and a rejection of mainstream conservative incrementalism.

bank.ctx: Donald Trump imposed 25% tariffs on Canadian goods and called Canada the 51st state. Mark Carney campaigned on economic sovereignty and won.
bank.q: Canada must diversify away from the United States. This is a national security issue, not just an economic one.

opts.t: Yes. Trade diversification and building domestic capacity is essential.
We should stand firm and negotiate hard, but ultimately the US relationship is irreplaceable.
Trade protectionism and economic nationalism will make Canadians poorer.

bank.q: Canada should never allow retaliation against the United States to trigger a trade war that hurts Canadian workers and families.

opts.t: Agreed. Our economic integration with the US is too deep for confrontation.
Counter-tariffs are a legitimate tool. You do not negotiate by showing weakness.
Canada must show it will not be bullied, regardless of the short-term costs.

bank.q: Axing the carbon tax is not climate policy. It is an election promise to people who do not believe in physics.

opts.t: Yes. The science and economics both support it.
The principle is sound but the rebate system has failed to bring public support.
No. It raises the cost of living for ordinary Canadians and should be eliminated.

bank.q: The carbon tax hits rural Canadians, farmers, and truckers hardest while doing almost nothing to change global emissions.

opts.t: Yes. It is a tax on people who have no alternatives.
The distributional impact is a real concern that should be addressed through better rebate design.
No. The rebate returns more to most households than they pay. The narrative is misleading.

bank.ctx: Average home price in Toronto: 1.1 million dollars. In Vancouver: 1.3 million. A generation has been priced out of the cities they grew up in.
bank.q: The housing crisis is a national emergency that requires the federal government to build at a scale not seen since the postwar period.

opts.t: Yes. Direct federal investment in affordable housing is the only answer.
Zoning reform and removing barriers to private construction is the right approach.
Both are needed. Supply reform and targeted public investment simultaneously.

bank.q: Immigration at current levels is driving up housing costs faster than we can build. Reducing intake is part of the housing solution.

opts.t: Yes. Supply cannot keep pace with demand at current immigration levels.
Build faster. Cutting immigration avoids the harder conversation about zoning and costs.
No. The housing crisis predates recent immigration surges. The cause is policy failure.

bank.ctx: Canada ranks last among peer countries on wait times. ER overcrowding. Family doctor shortages. 6 million Canadians with no primary care physician.
bank.q: The federal government must invest massively and immediately in healthcare or the public system will not survive.

opts.t: Yes. Healthcare is a right. Fund it properly.
Transfer more to provinces with strings attached. Outcomes-based funding.
The system needs structural reform, not more money poured into the same model.

bank.q: Allowing more private delivery of publicly funded healthcare services would reduce wait times without undermining the principle of universal access.

opts.t: Yes. The public system does not have to be the only system.
Some private delivery in specific areas is acceptable if strict safeguards prevent a two-tier system.
No. Private healthcare siphons doctors and creates inequality. The evidence is clear.

bank.q: Universal pharmacare would save Canadians billions in drug costs and leave no one choosing between food and medication.

opts.t: Yes. A single public payer for drugs is overdue.
Fill the gaps with targeted coverage for those who have nothing, not a full replacement of private plans.
No. Universal programs crowd out private insurance and reduce drug availability.

bank.q: The NDP-Liberal dental care program is a fiscal reckless expansion of government that Canada cannot afford.

opts.t: Yes. Adding new universal programs while the deficit grows is irresponsible.
The cost is real but dental care was a genuine gap in the social safety net.
No. Millions of Canadians could not afford dental care. This program is long overdue.

bank.ctx: Canada admitted over 400,000 new permanent residents in 2023, plus hundreds of thousands of temporary workers and students. Services and infrastructure are under strain.
bank.q: Canada grew too fast. Intake must come down sharply to let public services and housing catch up.

opts.t: Yes. The numbers were too high and must be reduced significantly.
Targeted reductions in specific categories, especially temporary foreign workers.
No. Canada needs immigrants to grow the economy and fund aging social programs.

bank.q: Canada has always been built by immigration. Turning against newcomers now is short-sighted and contrary to who we are.

opts.t: Yes. Our diversity is our greatest economic and cultural asset.
Immigration has been valuable but the pace and planning have been badly mismanaged.
Pride in immigration history does not mean unlimited present intake.

bank.ctx: Canada is the fourth largest oil producer in the world and has committed to net zero by 2050. The Trans Mountain expansion was completed in 2024.
bank.q: Canada cannot credibly claim to lead on climate while expanding fossil fuel infrastructure.

opts.t: Yes. New pipelines and oil sands expansion are incompatible with climate commitments.
A managed transition is needed. Abrupt shutdowns would devastate Alberta and workers.
Canadian oil is ethical and replacing it with imports would achieve nothing on climate.

bank.q: Alberta oil and gas workers and communities deserve the same respect that coastal or urban Canadians demand for their industries.

opts.t: Yes. The contempt shown for resource workers is a national unity problem.
Respect for workers is right. Defending the fossil fuel industry indefinitely is not.
Just transition programs must be fully funded and real, not symbolic.

bank.ctx: The Truth and Reconciliation Commission made 94 Calls to Action in 2015. Over a decade later, fewer than half have been fully implemented.
bank.q: Canada has failed Indigenous peoples again and again. Reconciliation requires action, not promises.

opts.t: Yes. Full implementation of the Calls to Action, treaty rights, and UNDRIP.
Progress has been slow but real. The process requires partnership, not speed.
Reconciliation should not override resource development that benefits all Canadians.

bank.q: The duty to consult with Indigenous communities should not give any group an effective veto over national infrastructure projects.

opts.t: Yes. One group should not be able to block projects that serve all Canadians.
Consultation must be meaningful, not a rubber stamp, but a veto is too far.
No. Indigenous land rights are treaty rights. Consent is not a veto, it is a right.

bank.ctx: Canada has run deficits since 2008. Federal debt stands at over 1.2 trillion dollars. Interest payments are now the fourth largest expenditure.
bank.q: Running deficits to invest in the future is responsible. Austerity now would be self-defeating.

opts.t: Yes. The deficit reflects necessary investment. Cut during a downturn and you cause one.
Deficits must come down, but not through cuts that harm services.
No. The trajectory is unsustainable. Future generations will pay for today.

bank.q: The capital gains inclusion rate hike punished entrepreneurs, investors, and anyone who built something. It should be reversed.

opts.t: Yes. It discourages risk-taking and investment in Canadian businesses.
Taxing capital gains more like income is fairer. The critics are protecting privilege.
A more targeted approach would be better than a blanket reversal.

bank.q: Canada should introduce a wealth tax on assets above 10 million dollars to fund public services and reduce inequality.

opts.t: Yes. Extreme wealth should contribute more to the society that made it possible.
The administrative challenges are real and the wealthy will find ways around it.
No. Wealth taxes drive capital and talent out of the country.

bank.q: Quebec has the right to hold a referendum on independence if its National Assembly votes for one.

opts.t: Yes. Self-determination is a democratic right.
Only with a clear question and a clear majority threshold, as the Clarity Act requires.
No. The 1995 vote settled this. Canada is not divisible.

bank.q: Federal spending in Quebec through the equalization formula subsidises policies that undermine national cohesion.

opts.t: Yes. Equalization requires reform to reflect contribution, not just need.
Equalization is a constitutional commitment. Attacking it threatens national unity.
The formula should be reformed to account for resource revenues more fairly.

bank.q: Gig workers deserve the same rights as employees. Uber drivers, couriers, and other platform workers should be classified as employees.

opts.t: Yes. The contractor model is a legal fiction used to deny rights.
A new category with some protections but not full employment rights makes more sense.
No. Flexibility is the point. Forced reclassification kills the model and the jobs.

bank.q: The federal government should use back-to-work legislation more readily to end strikes in essential services.

opts.t: Yes. The public cannot be held hostage by unions in critical sectors.
Back-to-work legislation is a last resort that undermines genuine bargaining.
No. It is union busting with government authority. Workers have a right to strike.

bank.ctx: Canada has committed to protecting 30% of land and water by 2030. It is currently at about 13%.
bank.q: Protecting nature is not a luxury. Biodiversity collapse is as serious a threat as climate change.

opts.t: Yes. Hit the 30x30 target. Old growth forests, wetlands, and coastlines must be protected.
Balanced approach. Conservation that works with, not against, resource communities.
Locking up 30% of Canada limits resource development and costs jobs.

bank.q: First-past-the-post must be replaced. Governments should not win massive majorities on a third of the vote.

opts.t: Yes. Proportional representation is long overdue.
Reform is needed but PR often produces unstable minority governments.
FPTP produces strong governments. The current system works well enough.

bank.q: Senate reform is long overdue. An appointed upper house with no democratic mandate is an embarrassment.

opts.t: Yes. Elect it or abolish it.
The independent appointment process is a reasonable compromise for now.
Senate abolition would require a constitutional amendment and is not worth the political cost.

bank.q: Canada should maintain a strong multicultural policy. Our diversity is a strength that should be actively supported by government.

opts.t: Yes. Official multiculturalism is a defining Canadian value.
Diversity is valuable but government promotion of cultural division is not.
Multiculturalism has undermined social cohesion. Integration, not celebration of separation.

bank.q: Medical assistance in dying has been expanded too far. Approving MAID for mental illness alone crosses a line.

opts.t: Yes. Mental illness MAID is dangerous and must not proceed.
Expansion should pause until safeguards are proven robust.
Autonomous individuals in severe, irremediable suffering have the right to choose.

bank.q: The federal government is too large, too expensive, and too intrusive. A smaller state would make Canada more free and more productive.

opts.t: Yes. Cut spending, reduce regulations, trust citizens to make their own decisions.
Some government is too large but public services and social programs are the right size or too small.
No. The state builds the foundations of prosperity. Cutting it cuts the floor out from under Canadians.

bank.q: The gap between the wealthiest Canadians and everyone else has grown to a level that threatens social cohesion and economic mobility.

opts.t: Yes. Progressive taxation, stronger social programs, and wealth taxes are needed.
Inequality is real but opportunity and growth matter more than redistribution.
Inequality reflects different efforts. Forced equality stifles ambition.

bank.q: Canada has under-invested in defence for decades. Meeting NATO commitments is both a security necessity and an alliance obligation.

opts.t: Yes. 2% of GDP is the floor, not the ceiling.
Smart defence spending yes, but raw percentage targets miss the point.
Canada should pursue peace through diplomacy, not military spending increases.

bank.q: Canada should prioritise its relationship with the US above all other foreign policy considerations, including relations with the EU and China.

opts.t: Yes. Geography and economics make the US relationship irreplaceable.
The US relationship is vital but over-dependence created our current vulnerability.
Canada must build a genuinely independent foreign policy centred on multilateralism.

bank.ctx: Grocery prices are up over 20% since 2021. The top five grocers posted record profits during the same period.
bank.q: Canadian grocers are profiteering and the government must act to stop it.

opts.t: Yes. Investigate, fine, and legislate if necessary.
Profits reflect inflation and supply chain costs, not gouging. More competition is the answer.
The government caused inflation through deficit spending. It should not pretend otherwise.

bank.q: The fastest way to reduce the cost of living is to reduce the regulatory and tax burden on businesses so they can compete and lower prices.

opts.t: Yes. Government regulation raises costs and limits competition.
Deregulation helps some costs but has a poor track record on consumer prices in concentrated markets.
No. Weak regulation is why grocers and telecom companies gouge Canadians.

bank.q: Canada is falling behind in artificial intelligence, biotech, and clean tech. The government must invest directly in strategic industries or lose out.

opts.t: Yes. Industrial strategy and public investment are how nations win.
Pick winners carefully, but the market is generally better at allocating capital.
Government picking winners is how you get Bombardier. Keep the state out of business.

bank.q: The government was right to require tech companies like Meta and Google to pay Canadian news publishers for journalism.

opts.t: Yes. News media is a public good and must be supported.
The principle is right but the execution caused Meta to block news entirely.
No. Government should not be subsidising and therefore influencing journalism.

bank.q: Canada defining itself primarily by opposition to American values is not a national identity. We need something more positive to rally around.

opts.t: Yes. Being not-American is thin and will not hold us together.
In the current moment, distinguishing ourselves from the US direction is essential.
Quebec and Indigenous nations both offer richer foundations for identity than pan-Canadian nationalism.

bank.q: Woke ideology has captured Canadian institutions - the CBC, universities, the civil service - and is making the country less free.

opts.t: Yes. There is an ideological conformism that does not tolerate dissent.
Some institutions lean left but the word woke is used to dismiss legitimate concerns.
No. This is a culture war import from the US and should be resisted.

bank.q: Shutting down Canadian resource industries to meet emissions targets just exports the pollution and the jobs to countries with lower standards.

opts.t: Yes. Canadian oil and mining operate to higher environmental standards than most alternatives.
The argument has some merit but cannot be used to justify unlimited extraction.
No. It is a fossil fuel lobby argument used to justify every new pipeline and mine.

bank.q: The CBC receives over 1.4 billion dollars in public funding annually. That money would be better spent elsewhere.

opts.t: Yes. Defund the CBC and let the market support journalism.
Reform it and reduce its budget but do not eliminate public broadcasting.
No. Public broadcasting is essential infrastructure for democracy and national identity.

bank.q: Donald Trump called Canada the 51st state. Any Canadian politician who does not treat that as a declaration of economic war is not fit for office.

opts.t: Yes. Canada must respond with full force - retaliatory tariffs, economic diversification, and a rejection of dependence.
No. Hardline nationalism in response to Trump plays into his hands. Smart negotiation wins more than gestures.

bank.q: Canada and the United States are deeply integrated economies. Treating our largest trading partner as an enemy would devastate Canadian workers.

opts.t: Yes. The economic relationship must be protected even when the politics are ugly.
No. Dependence on one country is the vulnerability. Build new markets now before the next crisis.

bank.ctx: 6 million Canadians have no family doctor. Emergency rooms are overwhelmed. Doctors and nurses are burning out and leaving.
bank.q: This is not a resource shortage. It is a political failure that has been building for 30 years.

opts.t: Yes. Fund it properly, train more doctors, and stop letting the system collapse.
No. More money without structural reform just feeds a broken system.

bank.q: Allowing more private clinics would cut wait times immediately. Refusing to consider it while people suffer is ideological stubbornness.

opts.t: Yes. Private clinics alongside a strong public system would help everyone.
No. Private clinics pull doctors from the public system and make wait times worse for those who cannot pay.

bank.q: Canada has apologised to Indigenous peoples many times and changed very little. Words without action are an insult.

opts.t: Yes. Full implementation of TRC Calls to Action, not another commission, not another apology.
No. The calls to action are being implemented gradually. Real change takes time.

bank.q: Every culture has produced things worth being proud of. I am proud of mine and I do not apologise for it.

opts.t: Yes. Pride in your own culture and heritage is natural and healthy.
Pride is fine but claims of superiority over others make me uncomfortable.
No. Ranking cultures above each other is where dangerous thinking begins.

bank.q: People alive today should carry a sense of shame for what their nation or culture did in the past - slavery, genocide, colonialism.

opts.t: Yes. Acknowledging inherited wrongs is part of being honest about who we are.
We should acknowledge history honestly without demanding personal shame.
No. You cannot be guilty of what you did not do. Collective guilt is unfair and divisive.

heroTitle: Where do you stand in Canadian politics?
heroSub: 47 policy issues and statements on the issues defining Canada right now - from the trade war with the US to housing, healthcare, and national identity. See your full party alignment.
partyStripHtml:
Liberal
Your answers most closely match the Liberal Party. You tend to support managed trade, social programs, climate action, and a strong federal role in the economy.
Conservative
Your answers most closely match the Conservative Party. You favour lower taxes, smaller government, axing the carbon tax, and reducing federal spending.
NDP
Your answers most closely match the NDP. You support pharmacare, dental care, workers rights, wealth taxes, and a more activist government on inequality and climate.
Green
Your answers most closely match the Green Party. You prioritise strong climate action, proportional representation, and a systemic challenge to corporate power.
Bloc Québécois
Your answers most closely match the Bloc. You support Quebec autonomy, French language protection, and a centre-left social model on federal matters.
PPC
Your answers most closely match the PPC. You want radical cuts to immigration, elimination of climate policy, and a rejection of mainstream conservative incrementalism.
